581	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
582	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
583	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
584	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
585	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
586	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
587	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
588	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
589	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
590	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
591	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
592	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
593	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
594	  p.~131.}
595	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
596	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
597	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
598	  \[
599	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
600	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
601	  \]
602	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
603	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
604	  the same definition specialises to
605	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
606	  sum over closed points.
607	\end{definition}
608	
609	\begin{theorem}
610	
611	\leanok
612	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
613	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
614	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
615	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
616	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
617	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
618	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
619	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
620	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
621	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
622	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
623	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
624	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
625	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
626	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
627	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
628	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
629	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
630	  \[
631	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
632	    \qquad
633	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
634	    \qquad
635	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
636	  \]
637	\end{theorem}
638	
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
641	  \leanok
642	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
643	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
644	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
645	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
646	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
647	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
648	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
649	  identities.
650	\end{proof}
651	
652	\begin{theorem}
653	
654	\leanok
655	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
656	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}

658	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
659	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
660	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
661	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
662	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
663	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
664	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
